Script_L7

Rata Rungsitiyakorn

2026-02-13

# This script shows how to detect and treat outliers in a
# salary dataset using boxplots, the IQR rule, and Z-scores,
# and compares the data before and after cleaning.

setwd("C:/Users/uSeR/Desktop/Jan2026/Lab7")

# 0) Load data and inspect
# Read salary dataset and check structure

comp <- read.csv("salary.csv")
str(comp)
## 'data.frame':    300 obs. of  5 variables:
##  $ age       : int  31 33 47 36 36 49 39 25 30 31 ...
##  $ experience: int  6 6 5 5 8 12 0 11 16 20 ...
##  $ gender    : chr  "Male" "Female" "Female" "Male" ...
##  $ department: chr  "Finance" "HR" "HR" "Marketing" ...
##  $ salary    : int  44707 51276 43379 37629 58273 59933 29566 60550 80353 80244 ...
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)
## Warning: package 'dplyr' was built under R version 4.1.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'dplyr'
## The following objects are masked from 'package:stats':
## 
##     filter, lag
## The following objects are masked from 'package:base':
## 
##     intersect, setdiff, setequal, union
library(gridExtra)
## Warning: package 'gridExtra' was built under R version 4.1.3
## 
## Attaching package: 'gridExtra'
## The following object is masked from 'package:dplyr':
## 
##     combine
# 1) Quick outlier check (Boxplot)
# Visual inspection of salary distribution

boxplot(comp$salary, main = "Salary (Check Outliers)")

ggplot(comp, aes(y = salary)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue") +
  theme_minimal()

# 2) IQR method for outlier detection
# Compute Q1, Q3, IQR and define lower/upper bounds

Q1 <- quantile(comp$salary, 0.25, na.rm = TRUE)
Q3 <- quantile(comp$salary, 0.75, na.rm = TRUE)
IQR_val <- IQR(comp$salary, na.rm = TRUE)

lower <- Q1 - 1.5 * IQR_val
upper <- Q3 + 1.5 * IQR_val

lower
##   25% 
## 17782
upper
##    75% 
## 103544
# 3) Identify outliers (IQR rule)
# Count and display rows outside the bounds

out_iqr <- comp$salary < lower | comp$salary > upper
sum(out_iqr, na.rm = TRUE)
## [1] 7
# show the outlier rows
comp[out_iqr, ]
##     age experience gender department salary
## 64   27         15   Male         HR 483357
## 71   31         22   Male         IT 104582
## 72   23          2   Male  Marketing 257523
## 127  37         22 Female         IT 106288
## 142  33         12   Male         HR 372106
## 236  24         14 Female    Finance 495684
## 259  34          9   Male    Finance 419405
# 4) Method A: Remove outliers
# Keep only data within IQR bounds

# Method A: remove outliers base R
comp_no_out <- comp[!out_iqr, ]
nrow(comp); nrow(comp_no_out)
## [1] 300
## [1] 293
# Method A: remove outliers dplyr
comp_clean <- comp %>% filter(salary >= lower & salary <= upper)

# Method B: winsorize/cap
#Cap extreme values to lower/upper bounds

comp_cap <- comp
comp_cap$salary <- pmin(pmax(comp_cap$salary, lower), upper)


# 3) Detect outliers using Z-score method

z <- as.vector(scale(comp$salary))
outlier_z <- abs(z) > 3
table(outlier_z)
## outlier_z
## FALSE  TRUE 
##   295     5
comp_clean_z <- comp[!outlier_z, ]
nrow(comp_clean_z)
## [1] 295
# 4) Compare before vs after cleaning
# Visual comparison using boxplots

# graph by base R
par(mfrow = c(1,2))
boxplot(comp$salary, main = "Before cleaning")
boxplot(comp_no_out$salary, main = "After (IQR removed)")

par(mfrow = c(1,1))

# graph by ggplots
p1 <- ggplot(comp, aes(y = salary)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "salmon") +
  labs(title = "Before cleaning") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(comp_no_out, aes(y = salary)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightgreen") +
  labs(title = "After removing outliers") +
  theme_minimal()

grid.arrange(p1, p2, ncol = 2)

# 5) Distribution plots: Histogram & Density (Before vs After)

h1 <- ggplot(comp, aes(x = salary)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5000, fill = "steelblue", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Histogram (Before)", x = "Salary", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

h2 <- ggplot(comp_no_out, aes(x = salary)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 5000, fill = "seagreen", color = "white") +
  labs(title = "Histogram (After IQR)", x = "Salary", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

d1 <- ggplot(comp, aes(x = salary)) +
  geom_density(fill = "steelblue", alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(title = "Density (Before)", x = "Salary", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

d2 <- ggplot(comp_no_out, aes(x = salary)) +
  geom_density(fill = "seagreen", alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(title = "Density (After IQR)", x = "Salary", y = "Density") +
  theme_minimal()

grid.arrange(h1, h2, d1, d2, ncol = 2)

# 6) Bar plot: Count by department

ggplot(comp, aes(x = department)) +
  geom_bar(fill = "orange") +
  labs(title = "Number of Employees by Department",
       x = "Department", y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

# 7) Boxplot & Violin: Salary by department

ggplot(comp, aes(x = department, y = salary)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightgray") +
  labs(title = "Salary by Department (Boxplot)",
       x = "Department", y = "Salary") +
  theme_minimal()

ggplot(comp, aes(x = department, y = salary)) +
  geom_violin(fill = "plum", alpha = 0.6) +
  labs(title = "Salary by Department (Violin)",
       x = "Department", y = "Salary") +
  theme_minimal()

# 8) Scatter plot: Experience vs Salary (+ regression line)

ggplot(comp_clean_z, aes(x = experience, y = salary)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE, color = "blue") +
  labs(title = "Salary vs Experience",
       x = "Experience (years)", y = "Salary") +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

# 9) Scatter with Facet: Compare by gender

ggplot(comp_clean, aes(x = experience, y = salary)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = FALSE) +
  facet_wrap(~ gender) +
  labs(title = "Salary vs Experience by Gender",
       x = "Experience (years)", y = "Salary") +
  theme_minimal()
## `geom_smooth()` using formula = 'y ~ x'

# 10) Boxplot + Jitter: See distribution and individual points

ggplot(comp_no_out, aes(x = department, y = salary)) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue", outlier.color = "red") +
  geom_jitter(width = 0.2, alpha = 0.4) +
  labs(title = "Salary by Department (Boxplot + Jitter)",
       x = "Department", y = "Salary") +
  theme_minimal()
